did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed a Computer-Aided Detection (CAD) system for classifying melanoma and nevus skin lesions using deep learning features extracted via a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with transfer learning, fused with handcrafted features using Mutual Information, and classified using SVMs, RVMs, and LR on the ISIC 2018 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a new Computer-Aided Detection (CAD) system for classifying melanoma and nevus skin lesions. It details a novel methodology involving the fusion of handcrafted features and deep learning features via Mutual Information, describes the experimental setup using the ISIC 2018 dataset, and reports specific performance results (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) compared to state-of-the-art methods. Although it includes a literature survey in Section 2, the primary contribution is the development and validation of a new technical approach, not a summary of prior literature.

title: Melanoma and Nevus Skin Lesion Classification Using Handcraft and Deep Learning Feature Fusion via Mutual Information Measures
brief_summary: This study presents a novel Computer-Aided Detection (CAD) system for classifying melanoma and nevus skin lesions by fusing handcrafted features based on the ABCD rule with deep learning features extracted via a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) using transfer learning. The feature fusion is performed using Mutual Information (MI) measurements to select the most significant features, which are then classified using Support Vector Machines (SVMs), Relevant Vector Machines (RVMs), and Linear Regression (LR). The proposed framework was evaluated on the ISIC 2018 public dataset, which contains over 10,000 dermoscopy images. The system achieved an accuracy of 92.40%, sensitivity of 86.41%, and an Imbalance Balanced Accuracy (IBA) of 0.80 on a balanced dataset. The results demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms several state-of-the-art approaches in terms of accuracy, specificity, and sensitivity. Additionally, the authors propose a novel procedure for adjusting evaluation metrics to better handle the imbalanced datasets common in skin lesion analysis.

lesion_types_studied.details: Used as one of the two classes in the binary classification task. The paper focuses on detecting 'dangerous skin lesions (melanoma type)' and explicitly includes MEL images from the ISIC 2018/HAM10000 dataset for the final experiment.
Used as the comparator class in the binary classification task. The study aims to differentiate melanoma from nevus lesions, including NV images from the ISIC 2018/HAM10000 dataset.
Mentioned as a class present in the original ISIC 2018/HAM10000 dataset but explicitly excluded from the study's binary classification experiment.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into Melanoma (malignant) or Nevus (benign) categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is to 'predict each unseen pigmented skin lesion image as a benign or malignant lesion' and modifies the ISIC 2018 dataset to perform a 'binary class classification' by excluding all classes except Melanoma and Nevus. The system uses classifiers (SVM, RVM, LR) to assign discrete diagnostic labels.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: The public ISIC 2018: Skin Lesion Analysis Towards Melanoma Detection grand challenge dataset (Task 3), also known as HAM10000. It contains dermoscopy images of pigmented skin lesions. The original dataset includes seven classes: Actinic Keratosis (AKIEC), Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC), Dermatofibroma (DF), Melanoma (MEL), Nevus (NV), Pigmented Benign Keratosis (BKL), and Vascular (VASC). For this study, the dataset was modified to include only Melanoma and Nevus classes for binary classification.

use.training: 75% of the dataset was used for training the classifiers (SVM, RVM, LR) after feature extraction and fusion.
use.testing: 25% of the dataset was used for testing the performance of the proposed CAD system.
use.other: The dataset was modified by excluding non-target classes (AKIEC, BCC, DF, BKL, VASC) to create a binary classification task. SMOTE oversampling was applied to handle class imbalance.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used as a fusion rule to select the most significant features from the combined set of handcrafted and deep learning features by measuring the relationship between datasets and discarding features with the lowest MI values.
Used to process the extracted features before classification to standardize the data distribution.

Applied to handle class imbalance in the dataset by oversampling the minority class (implied by the mention of adjusting evaluation metrics for imbalanced datasets and the use of Imblearn library, though specific statistical significance testing is not detailed).
Used as an evaluation metric to characterize classifier performance as a coefficient between predicted and observed elements of the binary classification.
Used as an evaluation metric to better handle the imbalanced datasets common in skin lesion analysis.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, etc.) and compared qualitatively to state-of-the-art methods.

ISIC 2018 (HAM10000).dataset_description: Binary classification of Melanoma (MEL) and Nevus (NV) lesions. Dataset modified to exclude other classes (AKIEC, BCC, DF, BKL, VASC). Split: 75% training, 25% testing. SMOTE oversampling applied for class imbalance.
ISIC 2018 (HAM10000).evaluation_setting: Comparison of various CNN architectures fused with handcrafted features using Mutual Information (MI) selection, classified via SVM, RVM, and LR.

Reference [24] (DermoDeep).notes: Used synthetic database constructed from four different databases.

ISIC 2018 (HAM10000).notes: The Mobilenet v2 architecture fused with handcrafted features demonstrated the best performance among the tested CNN architectures. The proposed method achieved an accuracy of 92.40%, sensitivity of 86.41%, and an IBA of 0.80 on the balanced dataset. The system outperformed Reference [18] and [22] in accuracy and sensitivity, though Reference [24] reported higher accuracy (95%) on a synthetic dataset.

architecture_used.main_model: Multiple CNN architectures used for feature extraction: VGG-16, VGG-19, MobileNet v1, MobileNet v2, ResNet-50, Inception v3, Xception, and DenseNet-201. The best performing model was MobileNet v2.
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained on ImageNet (ILSVRC ImageNet task) using transfer learning.
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported (ROI images extracted via boundary box algorithm after preprocessing).

architecture_details.feature_extraction: Deep learning features extracted from the selected CNN architectures. Number of features per architecture: VGG19 (4096), VGG16 (4096), ResNET-50 (2048), Inception v3 (2048), Mobilenet v1 (1024), Mobilenet v2 (1280), DenseNET-201 (1920), Xception (2048).
architecture_details.fusion_method: Fusion of deep learning features with handcrafted features (based on ABCD rule: Asymmetry, Borders, Colors, Dermatoscopic Structures) using Mutual Information (MI) measurements to select the most significant features.
architecture_details.handcrafted_features: Shape, color, and texture features (43 features total) derived from the Region of Interest (ROI).
architecture_details.feature_vector_size: 43 (handcrafted) + N (deep learning features), where N depends on the CNN architecture.

training_details.hardware: Intel Xeon E5 1230-V5 CPU, 32GB RAM, NVIDIA GeForce 1080Ti with 11 GB RAM
training_details.data_balancing: SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique) applied to handle class imbalance.
training_details.normalization: Z-score normalization applied to features before classification.

classifiers: Support Vector Machines (SVMs) with linear and RBF kernels

preprocessing_techniques: Gaussian filtering to blur artifacts such as hair, marks, and spots while maintaining lesion shape
Color space transformation to CIEL*a*b* to reduce channel correlation and preserve perceptual data
Mean thresholding applied to each channel (L, a*, b*) of the CIEL*a*b* space to differentiate skin tissue from lesion tissue
Logical intersection (AND operation) of binarized thresholded channels to form a binary mask
Median filtering (5x5 kernel) applied to the binary mask to remove remaining artifacts
Bounding box algorithm to compute the Region of Interest (ROI) and enclose the lesion
SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique) to handle class imbalance in the dataset
Z-score normalization applied to extracted features before classification

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to handle class imbalance by oversampling the minority class (melanoma) against the majority class (nevus). The method is based on K-NN clustering and Euclidean distance, selecting two points of the minority class and computing a new one based on them. It was applied iteratively until it reached an equivalent amount of information of the majority class.

conclusions: The proposed Computer-Aided Detection (CAD) system, which fuses handcrafted features (based on the ABCD rule) and deep learning features (extracted via CNNs) using Mutual Information (MI) measurements, achieved an accuracy of 92.40%, sensitivity of 86.41%, and an Imbalance Balanced Accuracy (IBA) of 0.80 on the balanced ISIC 2018 dataset.

Among the tested Convolutional Neural Network architectures (VGG16, VGG19, MobileNet v1, MobileNet v2, ResNet-50, DenseNet-201, Inception V3, and Xception), MobileNet v2 fused with handcrafted features demonstrated the best performance.
The proposed method outperformed several state-of-the-art methods in terms of accuracy, specificity, and sensitivity, including comparisons with Reference [18] and Reference [22].

While Reference [24] (DermoDeep) reported higher accuracy (95%), the authors argue that their results are more robust because they were obtained using the full ISIC 2018 database with data augmentation, whereas Reference [24] used a synthetic database constructed from preselected images from four different databases.

The fusion technique using Mutual Information effectively selects relevant features from both handcrafted and deep learning sets, offering a more informative weight justification compared to concatenation or discriminative analysis methods.

The proposed system enhances melanoma and nevus lesion recognition compared to fully deep learning approaches by incorporating medical-based dermoscopic features (ABCD rule), thereby avoiding the need for substantial amounts of labeled data and high computational costs associated with training deep networks from scratch.

The authors propose and justify a novel procedure for adjusting evaluation metrics, specifically the Imbalance Balanced Accuracy (IBA), to better handle the imbalanced datasets common in skin lesion analysis.

Extracting features from the Region of Interest (ROI) rather than the complete image guarantees that feature extraction is performed exactly on the lesion, reducing the probability of misclassification caused by healthy skin or artifacts.
important_remarks: The study modifies the ISIC 2018 (HAM10000) dataset by excluding five classes (AKIEC, BCC, DF, BKL, VASC) to create a binary classification task, which limits the generalizability of the results to multi-class clinical scenarios.

The dataset was artificially balanced using SMOTE oversampling; while this addresses class imbalance, it may lead to optimistic performance estimates compared to real-world clinical distributions where melanoma is the minority class.

The primary comparison against state-of-the-art method [24] (DermoDeep) is contested; the authors argue their results are more robust because they used the full ISIC 2018 database, whereas [24] used a synthetic database constructed from preselected images from four different databases, raising concerns about data leakage or selection bias in the baseline.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the significance of the performance differences between the proposed method and baselines.

The system relies on a specific preprocessing pipeline (Gaussian filtering, CIEL*a*b* transformation, mean thresholding, median filtering) to extract the Region of Interest (ROI); performance is highly dependent on the accuracy of this segmentation step, which may fail on images with poor quality or significant artifacts.
The study does not report external validation on independent datasets, limiting the assessment of the model's generalizability across different populations or imaging devices.

The proposed evaluation metric, Imbalance Balanced Accuracy (IBA), is a novel contribution of this work and has not been widely adopted or validated in the broader dermatology literature, making direct comparison with other studies difficult.

The computational cost of training the CNNs from scratch is avoided via transfer learning, but the fusion of handcrafted features (ABCD rule) with deep features adds complexity to the feature engineering process compared to end-to-end deep learning models.

dataset_usage_details.summary: The study utilized the ISIC 2018 (HAM10000) public dataset, modifying it to include only Melanoma (MEL) and Nevus (NV) classes for binary classification. The dataset was split into 75% for training and 25% for testing. SMOTE oversampling was applied to the training set to handle class imbalance. Deep learning features were extracted using CNNs pre-trained on ImageNet. No external validation or cross-dataset testing was performed.
dataset_usage_details.cross_dataset_validation_details: The paper did not perform cross-dataset validation. The model was trained and tested on splits of the same dataset (ISIC 2018).

training.notes: Used for training classifiers (SVM, RVM, LR) after feature extraction. SMOTE oversampling was applied to balance the classes within this split.

validation.notes: The paper does not report a separate validation set for model selection or hyperparameter tuning. It reports training and test performance.

testing.notes: Used for final performance evaluation of the proposed CAD system.

pretraining_or_transfer_learning.notes: Used to pre-train the CNN architectures (VGG16, VGG19, MobileNet v1/v2, ResNet-50, DenseNet-201, Inception V3, Xception) which were then used as feature extractors via transfer learning.

synthetic_or_augmented_data.notes: Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique (SMOTE) was used to oversample the minority class (Melanoma) in the training set to compensate for class imbalance.

evaluation_protocol.split_details: 75% training, 25% testing. The split was performed on the modified ISIC 2018 dataset containing only Melanoma and Nevus classes.
evaluation_protocol.unseen_data_details: No unseen external dataset was used. The test set was a held-out portion of the same source dataset.

dataset_level_results.notes: Results on the balanced test set. The model outperformed several state-of-the-art methods in accuracy and sensitivity.

generalizability_notes: The study lacks external validation on independent datasets, limiting the assessment of generalizability across different populations or imaging devices.
The dataset was modified to exclude non-target classes (AKIEC, BCC, DF, BKL, VASC), which limits generalizability to multi-class clinical scenarios.
SMOTE oversampling was used, which may lead to optimistic performance estimates compared to real-world clinical distributions.

The comparison with DermoDeep [24] is contested; the authors argue their results are more robust because they used the full ISIC 2018 database, whereas [24] used a synthetic database constructed from preselected images from four different databases.
information_not_reported: Exact number of images in the training and test splits (only percentages are given).
Patient-level vs. image-level split details (though image-level is implied by the dataset nature and lack of patient ID mention).
Specific hyperparameters used for the CNNs or SVM/RVM/LR classifiers.
Confidence intervals or p-values for the reported metrics.
